behaviors.description: `=SUM(A1:A10)` evaluates to the correct total for cells A1..A10.
behaviors.justification: The failure boundary is the handoff between range reference resolution and function evaluation. A pure `SUM(values[])` unit test can pass while range binding (`A1:A10`) is wrong.
behaviors.test_description: Create an in-memory sheet, set A1..A10 to known numbers, set B1 to `=SUM(A1:A10)`, run recalculation, and assert B1 equals expected sum. Include a case where one cell in the range changes and B1 updates.

behaviors.description: A formula referencing another formula cell computes the correct cascaded result.
behaviors.justification: This behavior is specifically about cross-cell formula chaining (`B1` depends on `A1`, `C1` depends on `B1`), which is a module-boundary interaction between dependency resolution and evaluator.
behaviors.test_description: Set `A1=3`, `B1==A1*2`, `C1==B1+1`; recalc and assert `B1=6`, `C1=7`. Then change `A1` and verify both recompute correctly.

behaviors.description: Circular dependency (A1↔B1) is detected and reported as an error.
behaviors.justification: Cycle detection only appears when dependency graph construction and evaluation interact; a single-function unit test can miss real graph wiring failures.
behaviors.test_description: Set `A1==B1+1` and `B1==A1+1`, trigger evaluation, assert a circular-reference error is emitted and evaluation terminates without hanging.

behaviors.description: `VLOOKUP` exact match returns correct value; missing key returns error.
behaviors.justification: Real failures often occur at the boundary between range extraction (table from cells) and VLOOKUP semantics. Testing only the lookup algorithm on arrays is insufficient for this behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Populate a lookup table range in cells, evaluate `VLOOKUP` for an existing key and a missing key, assert correct returned value and expected not-found error.

behaviors.justification: This is primarily expression semantics (conditional branching and nesting) within the evaluator/parser boundary, with no required external module interaction.
behaviors.test_description: Evaluate formulas like `=IF(A1>10, IF(A1>20, "high", "medium"), "low")` against representative `A1` values (e.g., 5, 15, 25) and assert outputs.

behaviors.description: `CONCATENATE` joins values with separator and skips empty cells.
behaviors.justification: This is function-level string semantics; no real failure boundary requires wiring multiple subsystems if inputs are supplied directly to evaluation.
behaviors.test_description: Evaluate concatenate expressions with normal and empty operands, asserting separator placement and that empty cells are skipped per spec.

behaviors.description: Cross-sheet reference (`Sheet2!A1`) resolves when sheet exists.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is cross-module name/sheet resolution plus evaluator handoff; unit-testing arithmetic alone cannot catch wrong sheet binding.
behaviors.test_description: Create workbook with `Sheet1` and `Sheet2`, set `Sheet2!A1`, set `Sheet1!B1==Sheet2!A1+1`, recalc, assert correct value.

behaviors.description: Dependent formulas recalculate in correct topological order after an input change.
behaviors.justification: This behavior is about dependency graph scheduling across cells, which is inherently multi-module and not a single local-function concern.
behaviors.test_description: Build a dependency chain/diamond graph of formulas, change a source cell, recalc, and assert final values match expected propagation order (no stale intermediate outputs).

behaviors.description: Undo after formula edit restores formula text and prior dependent computed values.
behaviors.justification: Requires interaction between undo/redo state management and recalculation engine. Unit-testing undo stack alone will miss dependent-value restoration failures.
behaviors.test_description: Set up dependent formulas, edit one upstream formula, verify changed results, perform undo, then assert original formula text and all downstream values are restored.

behaviors.description: Save to disk and load back preserves formulas, values, and floating-point precision.
behaviors.justification: Behavior explicitly depends on serialization/deserialization with real local filesystem I/O; mocks can hide binary format and precision round-trip defects.
behaviors.test_description: Create workbook with representative formulas and floating-point values, save to a temp file, reload, and assert formula text and numeric values match within strict tolerance (or exact bitwise where format guarantees it).
